title: Exact Single-Scale Outer Solution of the Abrikosov Vortex in the Extreme Type-II Limit
abstract: We determine the exact outer structure of the Abrikosov vortex in the extreme type-II limit, which occurs when the Ginzburg-Landau parameter $κ$ diverges. In this limit, Ginzburg-Landau theory simplifies, outside a shrinking core, to a closed nonlinear theory for the superfluid velocity subject to an algebraic density constraint. The resulting solution is asymptotically exact everywhere outside the vanishing vortex core, demonstrating that both magnetic field and superconducting density vary on the length scale of the London penetration depth. This establishes that the conventional two-length-scale picture of the vortex does not hold in the $κ\gg 1$ limit.
summary: This paper proves that in the limit of extremely high Ginzburg-Landau parameters, the Abrikosov vortex loses its two-scale structure (coherence length and penetration depth) and instead follows a single-scale pattern. By showing that the superconducting density is tied to the velocity field, the author provides an exact analytical solution for the vortex exterior. This challenges the standard assumption of two distinct length scales in extreme type-II superconductors.
main_problem: The paper investigates the structure of the Abrikosov vortex in the extreme type-II limit (where the Ginzburg-Landau parameter kappa approaches infinity) to determine if the conventional two-length-scale picture holds.
main_result: The study demonstrates that in the extreme type-II limit, the vortex outer structure is governed by a single length scale (the London penetration depth), as the superconducting density becomes algebraically slaved to the velocity field.
method: The author uses analytical derivation within the Ginzburg-Landau framework, employing an amplitude-phase representation and solving nonlinear differential equations using modified Bessel functions, supplemented by numerical verification.
model_or_system: The system is an Abrikosov vortex in a type-II superconductor described by the Ginzburg-Landau theory, which is mathematically equivalent to the static limit of the Abelian Higgs model.
key_observables: Superfluid velocity field, magnetic induction, superconducting density, and vortex line energy.
important_parameters: Ginzburg-Landau parameter kappa (specifically the limit kappa to infinity), coherence length, and London penetration depth.
main_assumptions: The solution is valid for the outer region of the vortex (r > 1/kappa) and assumes the extreme type-II limit where the vortex core shrinks to a point.
figures_summary: Figure 2 compares numerical Ginzburg-Landau solutions for various kappa values against the exact analytical Bessel-function profiles for magnetic induction and density deficit, showing uniform convergence.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of the extreme type-II limit, defines the Ginzburg-Landau model and the mathematical framework, derives the exact outer solution via a slaving condition, compares the results to the London model and numerical simulations, and discusses the implications of the single-scale structure.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Rescaled superconducting density R2 and rescaled magnetic induction b/b(0) as functions of the distance r (in units of the London penetration depth λ, logarithmic scale) from the vortex axis according to the GL equations (12) and (13) and κ = 20. The shaded region r ≲ξ, where ξ is the coherence length, is the vortex core. Textbook illustrations [3, 4] suggest distinct recovery scales for magnetic induction and superconducting density. In the extreme type-II limit, however, the exact outer solution, Eqs.(16) and (17), shows that both recover on the same scale outside a shrinking core.
FIG. 2. Convergence of the outer r > 1/κ GL solutions of the Abrikosov vortex, Eqs.(12), (13), to the exact κ →∞limit, Eqs.(16) and (17), shown as solid black curves.
